coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-embedded zinc-based metal-organic framework as an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of ferric and phosphate ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Changchang
Yang, Jie
Ni, Wang
Zeng, Wenyan
Xu, Jie
Zhang, Kedeng
Zhan, Xue-Qing
Ma, Ning
Tsai, Fang-Chang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots were prepared from urea and sodium citrate using a microwave method which was combined with zinc nitrate and dimethylimidazole to prepare CDs@ZIF-8 composites using a one-pot method. It was used to develop a novel fluorescent sensor for the rapid and effective detection of Fe3+. Owing to intense contacts and electron transfer between CDs@ZIF-8 and Fe3+, the fluorescence was severely diminished. The burst fluorescence of the Fe3+ system was restored considerably due to a higher binding preference between Fe3+ and PO4 3-. Furthermore, the detection limit for Fe3+ was 5.2 μM. Our construction of a fluorescent sensor provides a simple and rapid platform for detecting Fe3+ and PO4 3- in human health and biodiversity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ferric ion
Pyrophosphate ion
Detection
Fluorescent sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) (b) (a) The PXRD of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8; (b) The FT-IR of ZIF-8, CDs (c) (d)The FESEM of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8.

(a)(b) The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8; (c)(d) The pore size distribution of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8; (c) The zeta potential of CDs, ZIF-8and CDs@ZIF-8; (d) The TGA of CDs, ZIF-8and CDs@ZIF-8.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectrum of ZIF-8 and CDs; (b) The fluorescence emission spectrum of ZIF-8 and CDs under different excitation wavelengths;(c)(d) The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 (1 mg/mL) at different pH solutions; (e)(f) under different coexistence anions and linear fit of Fe3+concentration to fluorescence intensity ratio.

(a)(b) PL intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 in different metal cations and histograms of fluorescence intensity ratio (IF0-IF)/IF0 (The following fluorescence tests were performed in three parallel experiments, and the standard deviation is indicated in the bar chart.); (c) (d) PL intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 under Fe3+ with different coexistence metal ions in 1:1 ratio and histograms of fluorescence intensity ratio (IF0-IF)/IF0; (e) (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 in a gradient of Fe3+ concentration and linear fit of Fe3+concentration to fluorescence intensity ratio.

(a) (b) PL intensity of CDs-ZIF-8 (1 mg/mL) under different coexistence anions and histograms of fluorescence intensity ratio (IF0-IF/IF0) under Fe3+ with different coexistence anions in 1:1 ratio.; (c) The life time data of CDs, CDs-ZIF-8, CDs-ZIF-8-Fe (III) and CDs-ZIF-8-Fe (III) with phosphate; (d) The Schematic diagram of the response of fluorescent CDs to Fe3+ and PO4 3- (e)(f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-ZIF-8-Fe (III) (1 mg/mL) under different linear fit of phosphate ion concentration to fluorescence intensity ratio. (g) The fluorescence emission spectrum of the combination of various anions at the same concentration of 0.1 mM (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) with Fe3+ in a 1:1:1:1:1 ratio.
The synthetic schematic diagram of CDs@ZIF-8.
The comparison of Determination methods of Fe3+.

row.entry: Simple operation, low cost, low sensitivity.
High sensitivity, cumbersome operation and harsh conditions
High sensitivity, fast analysis speed, expensive instrument, high sample requirements
High sensitivity and rapidity, suitable for many types of samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe3+ by the proposed method.
Comparison of analytical capability with various methods.

row.entry: pH, various amino acids and ATP

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-embedded zinc-based metal-organic framework as an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of ferric and phosphate ions

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Data curation, Writing − original draft, Investigation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were prepared from urea and sodium citrate using a microwave method which was combined with zinc nitrate and dimethylimidazole to prepare CDs@ZIF-8 composites using a one-pot method. It was used to develop a novel fluorescent sensor for the rapid and effective detection of Fe3+. Owing to intense contacts and electron transfer between CDs@ZIF-8 and Fe3+, the fluorescence was severely diminished. The burst fluorescence of the Fe3+ system was restored considerably due to a higher binding preference between Fe3+ and PO4 3-. Furthermore, the detection limit for Fe3+ was 5.2 μM. Our construction of a fluorescent sensor provides a simple and rapid platform for detecting Fe3+ and PO4 3- in human health and biodiversity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic schematic diagram of CDs@ZIF-8 and the response of fluorescent CDs to Fe3+ and PO4 3-.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions play an essential role in human homeostatic mechanisms to imbalance many biological activities, involving respiration, photosynthesis, fixing nitrogen, DNA replication, oxygen transport, and electron transfer [8,10,17,30,39]. When iron deficiency occurs, the amount of hemoglobin synthesized by red blood cells decreases, resulting in a smaller volume of red blood cells and a decrease in oxygen-carrying capacity, leading to the formation of iron deficiency anemia [4]. Offering aquatic life with a capable, accurate Fe3+ detecting system is essential. Numerous methods exist for detecting Fe3+, including colorimetric approaches, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), etc. In practice, such methods have some drawbacks, including a protracted testing period, the requirement for complex sample preparation, and the high cost of laboratory equipment. In terms of sensitivity, strength, usability, and reaction time, analytical approaches based on fluorescent chemical sensors exceed traditional detection methods [12,52]. Phosphorus is an essential mineral element that is involved in almost all physiological chemical reactions in the human body. It is not only an important component of bones and teeth, but also participates in the control of acid-base equilibrium, energy metabolism and energy release in the body, and cell signaling [3,19,23,40]. In living organisms, the free phosphate ions that are present in solution are what constitute phosphorus. Water quality hygiene regulations require that phosphate levels be no greater than 0.05 mg/L [31]. Phosphate is also an essential anion in agricultural production. Erosion, surface runoff, industrial/agricultural effluent soil leaching, and dwelling sewerage are primary sources of phosphate entrance in the water cycle [22,29,34]. Excessive agricultural use of inorganic phosphate can generate an excess proliferation of algae, which may lead to the concentrations of dissolved oxygen to drop, culminating in red tide/water blooms[27,50]. Therefore, the design of luminescent and colorimetric sensors for effective monitoring of phosphate in the environment is of relevance.

Luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) are a kind of MOFs that exhibit appropriate luminescent behavior. The luminescent nature comes from the guest molecules interpenetrating the free space inside porous LMOFs[15,53]. Such interactions between guest molecules and the frameworks, primarily metal ions and LMOFs arouse alteration of the host LMOF's photophysical characteristics, changing the profiles of light absorption and emission. Through enhanced LMOF metal ion host-guest chemistry, this change in the host LMOF signal, also referred to as the response to metal ions, can detect specific metal ions. Razavi, Morsali [32,36,47,53]. The methods to enhance the luminescence properties of MOFs with encapsulated carbon spots are (1) through putting an encircling effect towards guests, that reduces the non-radiative rot of guests and boosts their quantum yield; (2) by lessening the ACQ effect, which enables a significant number of guests who can achieve luminescence in the solid-state [11,21,49]; (3) MOFs, like ZIF-8, UiO-66 [33,45], possess superb thermal and water-based stability, making it conceivable to use these as ‘shields’ to boost the stability of the restricted guests. (F. [43]). Carbon dots are one type of fluorescence carbon nanomaterials, which have various benefits: extraordinary photoluminescence capabilities, exceptional dissolution, low toxicity, ultrahigh specific surface area, chemical inertness, and good biocompatibility [1,2,6,16,28,37]. Aside from that, the carbon's surface contains functional groups including amino, carboxyl, and others creating the prerequisites for the function applications (F. [43,52]). Nonetheless, the inhibition of fluorescence brought by the CDs agglomeration probably restricts whose sensing applies owing to molecular interactions or the creation of a radical complex that is not stimulated by radiation. Deng et al., [9,44]. Consequently, the rigid structure of MOFs can be regarded as a stable host to prevent the accumulation of CDs.

The CDs@ZIF-8 we used can detect Fe3+ and PO4 3- continuously, and metal ions can be detected by quenching signals (fluorescence quenching) or quenching on fluorescent detector nanoplatforms. Additionally, the CDs@ZIF-8 has good stability, is unaffected by pH or anion response, and exhibits particular selectivity for Fe3+ in common metal cation response assays, making it an efficient tool for the detection of Fe3+ and PO4 3-. Hu et al., [18,26,41,48,51].

Herein, CDs exhibiting a strong blue emission were prepared by using microwave treatment. A one-pot method was applied to synthesize CDs@ZIF-8 using carbon dots, zinc nitrate, and 2-methylimidazole. ZIF-8 effectively prevents carbon aggregation and stabilizes carbon nanostructures to lessen their ACQ effect because of the pore structure and particular binding sites. CDs@ZIF-8 has good stability, is unaffected by pH and anion reaction, and has great specific selectivity for Fe3+ in common metal cation reaction tests, allowing it to be employed as a fluorescence sensor to detect Fe3+. The synergistic effect of CDs and MOFs in CDs@MOFs has been widely applied in photocatalytic degradation of metal ions and environmental contaminants, as well as heavy metal ion fluorescence sensing. Then, CDs@ZIF-8 can realize the continuous on-off-on response of Fe3+ and PO4 3-, which is promising as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of iron and phosphate ions in aquatic environments. The ability of CDs@ZIF-8 to detect Fe3+ and PO4 3- in aquatic environments as a fluorescence sensor seems promising. Table 1.

2,4-Dimethylimidazole was bought from Aladdin. urea, citric acid, Zinc nitrate hexahydrate, and EtOH were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. The entire experiment was conducted with deionized water. Every reagent was of analytical quality.

Using a Bruker D8A25 (air ambient) powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) with a scanning range of 5–50 °, the phase composition and crystal structure of materials were examined. With a temperature range of 30–800 °C and a ramp rate of 10 °C/min, the Mettler Toledo TGA1 was used to measure the thermogravimetric analysis. Using Nicolet-iS 50 to acquire the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. After degassing at 150 °C, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm was evaluated utilizing the specific surface area and pore size analyzer (QDS-MP-30). Zetasizer Nano ZS90, a tool for measuring nanoparticle dimensions and potential, was obtained to determine the zeta potential. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was captured using an F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer at a 365 nm excitation wavelength. And the excitation slit sample is both 10 nm, with 1200 nm/min.

Using a microwave reaction, CDs were created. In 10 mL de-ionized (DI) water, 0.3 g urea and 0.5 g citric acid were evenly distributed. The resulting homogenous solution was then microwaved for 5 min. After 20 mL of water had been added to the mixed liquor filter, it was passed again with a 0.22 µm microporous filter to get rid of any leftover impurities. Finally, a 500 Da dialysis bag was used to dialyze the pure CDs for 24 h. And for the upcoming experiment, the CDs solution was frozen-dried.

The hydrothermal approach is used in the Synthesis of ZIF-8 to cite prior relevant publications. 11.75 mg Zinc nitrate hexahydrate and 2.27 g 2-Methylimidazole were scattered in the 10 mL H2O, then kept stirring for 0.5 h in a water bath kettle at 25 °C. Following the reaction, the product was rinsed three times with ethanol and water separately. Then it was dried for 8 h at 40 °C in a vacuum oven.

The one-pot approach was prepared to be utilized by CDs@ZIF-8. 11.75 mg ZnNO3·H2O and 2.27 g 2-Methylimidazole were dispersed and dissolved by ultrasound in the 10 mL CDs, then the obtained homogeneous solution was stirred at 0.5 h in the water bath kettle. After being washed three times with DI water, once with ethanol, and then vacuum dried for a whole night at 60 °C, the CDs@ZIF-8 were obtained.

Scheme 1 displays the synthetic schematic of CDs@ZIF-8. Firstly, CDs are formed by microwave method. Sequentially, a One-pot method of CDs@ZIF-8 was applied. Because these activities dramatically reduce CDs by producing radical complexes that aren't triggered by radiation or by quenching luminescence as a result of aggregation. Therefore, the distribution of CDs in ZIF-8 can address other issues as well as the quenching issue caused by CDs aggregation. In addition, to find heavy metal ions, CDs@ZIF-8 can be utilized as a fluorescence sensor. Data analysis of the distribution of the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 was done to characterize the CDs and demonstrate that they were successfully complexed with ZIF-8.

Fig. 1a shows the PXRD patterns of the prepared materials. They are in good agreement with the predicted one obtained from the ZIF-8 one-crystal structure, demonstrating the correctly synthesized CDs and ZIF-8. As not any additional peaks appeared, the present synthetic approach was successful in producing the CDs@ZIF-8 structure. Yang, Yan [46]. It is intuitively shown that, under the PXRD pattern, the encapsulating of CDs does not affect the topographical structure of ZIF-8. The samples are measured using FT-IR to further track the development of the material fabrication process. The spectra of prepared materials are each characterized independently, as shown in Fig. 1b. Without a doubt, the vibrational stretches of CDs v(N-H) are what causes the absorption peak near 3400 cm−1. The matching ZIF-8 infrared absorption spectra showed the peaks at 2931 cm−1 associated with the methyl group. N atom is employed for the Zn-N-C and CN-C bonds., so there is no N-H bonding in ZIF-8. The graph indicates that the CDs@ZIF-8 has an N-H bond absorption peak at the same location as CDs. The FT-IR Spectrogram demonstrates that the material has multiple unique bands linked to CDs and pure ZIF-8 crystal components, proving that the CDs@ZIF-8 was efficiently synthesized. Fig. 1c, d shows how the FESEM was applied to analyze the morphology of CDs@ZIF-8. Furthermore, ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 have prismatic dodecahedron morphologies.

The porosity and specific surface area materials were evaluated using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, and the findings are depicted in Fig. 2a, b. ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 exhibit Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas of 1331.16 and 1207.90 m2•g−1. It was further demonstrated CDs were effectively enclosed within the pore of ZIF-8 by the fact that the specific surface area of the composite reduced considerably when compared to pure ZIF-8. The non-local density functional theory model was used to calculate the pore size distribution, which points to these two materials as having extremely tiny micropore dispersals at 0.737 nm. The network that forms between Zn2+ and the ligands is thought to be responsible for the dispersal of pore sizes. Zeta potential measurements are made to further verify the protective covering of CDs. Fig. 2e displays the electronegativity of materials. With CDs enclosed, the surface charge of ZIF-8 rises. This event shows that the CDs are trapped inside perforations of MOF. Additionally, a nitrogen dioxide-rich environment was used for the TGA measurement to verify the samples' thermal stability. Starting at 200 °C and peaking at roughly 400 °C, this step represents the early disintegration stage of CDs@ZIF-8 in Fig. 2 f. And temperatures required to decompose were roughly 580 °C and 600 °C, respectively. The chart shows that approximately 27.4 wt% of CDs are mass-encapsulated in ZIF-8. This further demonstrated how effectively CDs and ZIF-8 were prepared. Guo et al., [14].

Firstly, fluorescence experiments were run on aqueous solutions of Composite Materials to evaluate the luminous characteristics of the synthetic samples in Fig. 3a. Near the emission spectra of CDs in H2O, which is centered between 450 and 670 nm with a height peak of 500 nm, ZIF-8 exhibits modest fluorescence at a wavelength of 470 nm. Following that, various excitation wavelengths of 365 nm and 400 nm were used to evaluate the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs@ZIF-8 composite. ZIF-8 exhibits more excellent fluorescence when CDs are encapsulated. A large emission peak is visible in the CDs@ZIF-8 in the case of an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, which also causes it to glow blue. As a result, further fluorescence experiments were conducted in an aqueous solution and at 365 nm excitation frequency. Each sample was prepared in two batches, and the test was repeated three times.

Due to the carbon surface with amino, which can be used to enrich metal ions by complexing with them to detect metal ions in aqueous solutions. As discovered in Fig. 3, it is obvious that under different pH, IZIF-8/ICDs essentially remained steady within a slight variation in a pH value of 7 (IF0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 a pH value of 7, and IF represents the fluorescence intensity in different pH solutions). It indicates that different pH solutions do not have a great impact on the fluorescence intensity ratio. The preparation of aqueous solutions with various pH values involves diluting sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid. In addition, the CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescence intensity variations have been detected in numerous common anion solutions. IZIF-8/ICDs in Cl-, HCO3 -, PO4 -, and NO3 - anion solution hardly changes. It illustrates the CDs@ZIF-8 composite's excellent stability in a range of pH and anion solutions and establishes a rationale for its application as a luminous ratio sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8 with Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Subsequently, the response of CDs@ZIF-8 to metal cations and its anti-interference ability were studied. In an ethanol solution, CDs@ZIF-8 was disseminated with several metal ion, including Na+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Ni2+. All the metal ions were set to 1 mM. For the subsequent assays, a composite with a concentration of 1 mg/mL was utilized. The fluorescence intensity responses were recorded in Fig. 4a. Fe3+ greatly reduced the brightness of the composites in Fig. 4b when compared to other metal cations. We discovered that Fe3+ gave the same level of quenching effect when combined with other coexisting metal ions in a 1:1 ratio, demonstrating that the interfering chemicals had no impact on the Fe3+ determination. It can be concluded from the fluorescence lifetime results that the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs@ZIF-8 is the same as that of CDs@ZIF-8-Fe3+ and CDs@ZIF-8-Fe3+-PO4 3-. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching induced by Fe3+ and PO4 3- may be the result of static quenching. In general, static quenching occurs when the quencher strongly interacts with the fluorophore to form a non-fluorescent ground state complex [20,24,38]. The change of ground state will cause the disturbance of the absorption spectrum of fluorophore.
(1) The emission intensity at 365 nm decreased as the Fe3+ concentration increased, as illustrated in Fig·4e, f, when the Fe3+ concentration was between 0·1 and 1 mM·LOD = 3σ/K
where σ is the standard deviation of the initial intensities of samples for five blank measurements without explosives; K is the slope of the linear intensity–concentration curve.

The luminescence quenching constants (Ksv) were quantitatively calculated using the Stern–Volmer equation:
(2) I0/I = 1 + KSV C
where I and I0 are luminescence intensities of samples with or without explosives, respectively; C is the concentration of Fe3+ in mM.

The static quenching equation accurately measured the proportionality connection between relative fluorescence intensity (IF0-IF/IF) and concentrations:
IF0-IF/IF0=1·02558[C0]−0·01837
The RSD was 0.22%− 0.79% ( Table 2). It indicated the reliability of Fe3+ detection by this method.

Where [C0] is the concentration of Fe3+ with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9954. IF0 and IF denote the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of Fe3+. According to the formula, the S/N (signal-to-noise) ratio of CDs@ZIF-8 for Fe3+ is 3, and the detection threshold is 5.2 μM. And we enumerate the analytical capability compared with various methods in Table 3. Iron ion concentration meets the government standard (GB 5749–2006), which sets the allowable amount of Fe3+ in drinking water at 0.3 mg/L.

Interestingly, contrary to the addition of other anions (such as SO4 2-, HCO3-, SO3 2-, Cl-, NO3-), the addition of PO4 3- at a ratio of 1:1 to CDs@ZIF-8 containing Fe3+ resulted in a notable restoration of the fluorescence intensity. It is demonstrated that PO4 3- spontaneously interacts with Fe3+ to create the complex, which is the explanation for the aforementioned phenomena, as shown in Fig. 5a, b. PO4 3- spontaneously bonds to Fe3+ to form a complex, and the ability of the complex to bind to iron ions is stronger than the ability of Fe3+ to bind to PO4 3-, causing Fe3+ to bind to PO4 3- to form a more stable substance. By doing so, the CD can be expelled from the body and part of the fluorescence can be recovered. For CDs, CDs-ZIF-8, CDs-ZIF-8-Fe3+, and CDs-ZIF-8-Fe3+ with phosphate, the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were 8.83 ns, 5.14 ns, 5.05 ns, and 5.09 ns. In Fig. 5c, CDs, CDs-ZIF-8, CDs-ZIF-8-Fe (III), and CDs-ZIF-8-Fe (III) with PO4 3-had fluorescence lifetimes of 8.83 ns, 5.14 ns, 5.05 ns, and 5.09 ns, respectively. Fe3+ addition had little to no effect on the fluorescence lifetimes of complexes, which is consistent with static quenching. The Stern-Volmer equation still uses it. The concentration of PO4 3- gradually raises the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8-Fe3+, and there is still a strong linear association between IF0-IF)/IF0 and PO4 3- concentration. The static quenching equation is obtained by linear fitting.
(IF0-IF)/IF0=0·54927[C0]+0·28063

In this formula, [C0] represents the concentration of PO4 3-, and its correlation coefficient R2 is 0.99203. According to the IUPAC standard, the detection limit is 0.77 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. To some extent, CDs@ZIF-8 can achieve sequential detection of Fe3+ and PO4 3- in the future.

Fig. 4c and d show that the composites are interference-resistant in a variety of cationic solutions (including Na+, Mg2+, K+, CO2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, and Ca2+). The preparation of a combination of various ions at the same concentration of 0.1 mM (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) with Fe3+ in a 1:1:1:1:1 ratio was used to conduct fluorescence tests in Fig. 5 g. The static equation for Fe3+ detection that was produced took the data into account, and the Fe3+ concentration of 0.020203 mM was calculated. This is consistent with the actual concentration of Fe3+ injected, which was 0.02 mM, and thus supports the use of the composite material as a fluorescence sensor in practical detection.

In summary, we designed a brand-new fluorescence sensor. Through a straightforward one-pot method, water-soluble CDs were encased in luminous MOF. Additionally, in an aqueous solution, the CDs@ZIF-8 demonstrated strong structural and fluorescence stability. Additionally, CDs@ZIF-8 has a linear range of 0.1–1 mM and a low LOD of 5.2 μM, making it an effective and specific fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+. Additionally, the effects of both internal and external parameters, such as pH and background fluorescence, were insignificant or barely noticeable. And the combination of iron ions and phosphate ions resulted in a significant change in fluorescence intensity. Finally, combining CDs with ZIFs as a functional MOF-based fluorescence sensor offers a quick and precise method for measuring Fe3+ and PO4 3-, which would be of great significance to human health and biodiversity.

Changchang Zhu: Data curation, Writing − original Draft, Investigation. Jie Yang: Visualization, Validation. Wang Ni: Data curation. Wenyan Zeng: Investigation. Jie Xu: Investigation. Kedeng Zhang: Validation. Xue-Qing Zhan: Methodology. Ning Ma: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing. Fang-Chang Tsai: Supervision, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52203161); Key Research and Development Plan of Hubei Province (2022BAD029); Technical Innovation and Development Project of Hubei Province (High-tech Enterprises) (2021BAB085); Supported by Open Funding Project of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2021031).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur-doped carbon dots@polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes for dual-modality detection of norovirus

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Achadu, O.J., Abe, F., Hossain, F., Nasrin, F., Yamazaki, M., Suzuki, T., & Park, E.Y. (2021). Sulfur-doped carbon dots@polydopamine-functionalized magnetic silver nanocubes for dual-modality detection of norovirus. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 193, 113540. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2021.113540.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent innovations of nanotechnology in water treatment: A comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ajith, M.P., Aswathi, M., Priyadarshini, E., & Rajamani, P. (2021). Recent innovations of nanotechnology in water treatment: A comprehensive review. Bioresource Technology, 342, 126000. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2021.126000.
Brown, R.B., & Razzaque, M.S. (2018). Phosphate toxicity and tumorigenesis. Biochim Biophys Acta Rev Cancer, 1869(2), 303–309. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbcan.2018.04.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen doped carbon nanodots as fluorescent probes for selective detection and quantification of Ferric(III) ions
